ks1.objective: Tag others without being tagged. If you're tagged, take a knee until the player who got you is tagged out.
successCriteria: Re-enters when their tagger goes out

lks2.objective: Develop honesty, scanning and quick footwork in a self-managed tag game.
successCriteria: Reads the area for the player who tagged them

uks2.objective: Combine self-refereeing with dribbling — manage honesty, the ball and the chase.
successCriteria: Models honest behaviour for younger players

space.description: A square playing area suitable for the size and age of the group. Begin without footballs to establish understanding of the rules.

value.name: Responsibility
value.text: Responsibility through honesty and integrity. Responsibility means telling the truth, even when it doesn't benefit you. In this game there's no referee — players manage it themselves. If you're tagged, it's your responsibility to admit it straight away and take a knee without arguing. The game works best when everyone is honest.

steps: All children are taggers — aim to tag one another while avoiding being tagged yourself.
If a child is tagged, they immediately take a knee or sit where they were tagged.
They can only re-enter the game once the player who tagged them has been tagged by someone else.
If two children tag each other at the same time, they play a quick game of Rock, Paper, Scissors to decide who takes a knee.
Allow the game to flow with minimal coach intervention — children are responsible for managing honesty and fairness.
progressions: Introduce footballs so children must dribble while playing.
Allow children to re-enter the game after completing a short ball-mastery task (e.g. 5 toe taps).
Increase the difficulty of the re-entry task over time — 10 toe taps, 3 keepy-ups.
questions: How does it feel to own being tagged straight away?
What happens to the game when people don't admit they were tagged?
How do you keep track of who tagged you?

step.space: Smaller area = more traffic + scanning; bigger area = speed + endurance
step.task: Top performers use weaker foot only; specific surfaces of the foot when travelling
step.equipment: Add gates around the area children must dribble through before tagging
step.people: Pair children up so they work together as a team

base.showmanship: Praise children loudly when they admit being tagged without hesitation. Use language like 'That's great responsibility' or 'You owned that straight away, well done!'
